task: I’m considering leaving a rental in someone else’s hands for a stretch, and I want to know what records, updates, or inspection points would make it easier to tell whether things are being handled properly. What would you want in place before stepping back from day-to-day oversight?

turns.agent_response:
Before stepping back, I’d want a simple system that gives you **proof, visibility, and accountability** without needing to micromanage.

## 1) A baseline “handoff” record
Have a clear snapshot of the rental before you step back:
- Date-stamped photos/video of every room, exterior, appliances, meters, and any existing damage
- A written condition report
- Copies of leases, addenda, keys/access codes, warranties, manuals
- Utility account info and vendor contacts
- List of recurring obligations: rent collection, inspections, lawn/snow, filters, pest control, etc.

## 2) A regular reporting cadence
Ask for updates on a schedule, even if nothing is happening:
- **Monthly summary**: rent status, occupancy, maintenance issues, expenses, and cash flow
- **Work order log**: what came up, when it was reported, who handled it, cost, and completion date
- **Lease/compliance updates**: renewals, notices, late payments, violations, insurance changes
- **Photos on request or at set intervals** for vacant units or during turnover

A short standardized report is better than long informal texts.

## 3) Inspection checkpoints
You want a few objective check-ins:
- **Move-in / move-out inspection**
- **Mid-lease inspection** or periodic property walkthroughs
- **Vacancy inspection** if a unit is empty
- **Post-repair verification** with before/after photos
- **Annual safety/compliance inspection**: smoke/CO detectors, HVAC, plumbing leaks, electrical issues, locks, trip hazards, mold signs, exterior condition

If you’re leaving for a long stretch, I’d want at least one scheduled inspection every 3–6 months, depending on property type and tenant situation.

## 4) Financial transparency
Require records that let you verify money is being handled properly:
- Rent roll and payment ledger
- Bank statements or owner portal access
- Copies of invoices, receipts, and paid bills
- Security deposit accounting
- Reserve balance and any emergency spending threshold
- Clear approval limits: e.g. manager can approve repairs up to $X, above that they must call you

## 5) Maintenance accountability
For each issue, you want:
- Date reported
- Severity/priority
- Temporary mitigation if needed
- Vendor selected and why
- Estimated vs actual cost
- Completion confirmation
- Follow-up if the repair failed

This prevents “we looked at it” from counting as “it’s fixed.”

## 6) Tenant communication log
Keep a record of:
- Complaints
- Notices sent
- Lease violations
- Repair requests
- Response times
- Any promises made to tenants

That helps you see whether communication is timely and consistent.

## 7) Emergency and escalation plan
Before stepping back, define:
- What counts as an emergency
- Who can act immediately
- When you must be contacted
- Backup contacts if the first person is unavailable
- Insurance claim steps
- Contractor authorization rules

## 8) Access and security controls
Make sure you know:
- Who has keys/codes
- How access is tracked
- How locks/codes are changed after turnover
- Whether cameras are used, and where
- How mail, packages, and vacant-unit access are handled

## 9) A simple KPI-style dashboard
If you want a quick read on whether things are being handled well, track:
- Vacancy rate
- Rent collection rate
- Average maintenance response time
- Number of open work orders
- Delinquency rate
- Repair costs vs budget
- Inspection findings unresolved after 30 days

## 10) A written authority agreement
If someone else is managing it, get a written agreement covering:
- Scope of authority
- Spending limits
- Reporting frequency
- Inspection schedule
- Recordkeeping requirements
- Termination/handback process

### If I were setting this up, I’d want:
1. A move-in baseline report  
2. Monthly financial + maintenance reports  
3. Scheduled inspections with photos  
4. Repair logs with receipts  
5. Clear spending/approval limits  
6. A written escalation plan  

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** or a **property oversight template** you can hand to a manager or family member.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it tighter and more enforceable.

What you listed is the right shape. I’d add a few things that catch people when they’re sloppy or trying to hide problems:

- **Meter readings** at handoff and after any vacancy. Water, gas, electric. Stops arguments.
- **A dated photo set for every inspection**, not just move-in. Same angles if possible.
- **Repair threshold in writing** — what they can spend without calling me, and what needs my approval.
- **Receipts within 48 hours** for any expense over a small amount. No “I’ll send it later.”
- **Tenant notices saved** exactly as sent. Email, text, paper — whatever was used.
- **Vacancy/turnover checklist** if a unit opens up: locks, smoke detectors, leaks, appliances, carpet, trash, odors, keys returned.
- **A monthly exception report**, not just a summary. I don’t care if “nothing happened” — I want to know what changed.

And I’d keep one rule simple: if they can’t produce records fast, they probably don’t have them.

I’ve seen this kind of thing go bad when people rely on “the manager said” instead of paper. Last rental I had, that was exactly the problem — fees were getting taken, but nobody was watching the actual condition until it was already expensive. So I’d rather have too much documentation than not enough.

If you want, I can turn this into a plain checklist with sections for **before handoff, monthly, and vacancy/repair**.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d tighten it into something that’s hard to fake and easy to audit.

## Before stepping back
Require a **baseline handoff packet**:
- Date-stamped photos/video of every room, exterior, appliances, meters, and existing damage
- Written condition report
- Meter readings: water, gas, electric
- Copies of leases, addenda, warranties, manuals
- Keys, codes, access list
- Vendor contacts and utility/account info

## Every inspection
Use the same format each time:
- **Dated photo set every inspection**
- Same angles where possible
- Notes on anything changed since last time
- Meter readings if vacant or if usage looks off

## Repairs and spending
Make authority explicit:
- **Repair/spending threshold in writing**
- What they can approve without calling you
- What requires your approval first
- **Receipts within 48 hours** for any expense over a set amount
- Before/after photos for repairs

## Tenant and legal records
Keep exact copies of:
- All tenant notices, exactly as sent
- Emails, texts, letters, delivery proof
- Complaints, violations, late notices
- Lease renewals and move-in/move-out paperwork

## Vacancy / turnover checklist
If a unit opens up, require a checklist with:
- Locks and keys
- Smoke/CO detectors
- Leaks, moisture, odors, pests
- Appliances and HVAC
- Carpet/flooring/walls
- Trash removal and cleaning
- Final meter readings

## Monthly reporting
Don’t accept just a narrative. Ask for:
- Rent roll
- Open work orders
- Expenses and receipts
- Delinquencies
- What changed this month
- **Monthly exception report**: anything unusual, overdue, broken, disputed, or pending

## One simple rule
If they can’t produce records quickly, assume the records don’t exist.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page enforceable checklist** with:
1. handoff requirements  
2. monthly reporting requirements  
3. vacancy/repair requirements  
4. approval limits and deadlines

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little too tidy, though.

What I’d want next is the part that makes it enforceable on paper, not just a nice checklist:
- who gets the reports
- exact due dates
- what happens if a report is late or incomplete
- what counts as a breach
- how long they keep records
- how fast they have to answer if I ask for backup

I’d also want a hard rule on storage. One folder, one system, no scattered texts and side emails unless they’re copied in. Otherwise people start cherry-picking later.

And for inspections, I’d want the same checklist every time, no improvising. If the angle changes, the picture isn’t worth much.

Make it into something I could hand to a manager or family member and say: this is the standard, or don’t bother.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — at that point you want a **written operating standard**, not just a checklist.

Here’s a version you could hand to a manager or family member.

---

# Rental Oversight Standard

## 1) Purpose
This standard sets the minimum required records, inspections, reporting, and response times for managing the property. If these requirements are not met, management is not being performed to standard.

## 2) Reporting recipients
All required reports must be sent to:
- Property owner: [name/email]
- Backup contact: [name/email]
- Any additional recipient: [name/email]

No report is complete unless it is sent to all required recipients.

## 3) Required reporting schedule
### Monthly report
Due by: **[day] of each month**
Must include:
- Rent roll
- Delinquencies
- Open work orders
- Expenses
- Receipts
- Vacancies
- Lease issues
- Any unusual events
- Monthly exception report

### Inspection report
Due within: **24 hours of each inspection**
Must include:
- Same checklist every time
- Dated photos
- Notes on changes since last inspection
- Meter readings if applicable

### Repair report
Due within: **48 hours of completion**
Must include:
- Issue reported
- Action taken
- Vendor used
- Cost
- Before/after photos
- Receipt

## 4) Late or incomplete reports
A report is considered **late** if it is not delivered by the deadline.

A report is considered **incomplete** if it is missing any required item.

If a report is late or incomplete:
- It must be corrected within **2 business days**
- The omission must be explained in writing
- Repeated late or incomplete reporting is a breach of standard

## 5) What counts as a breach
Any of the following is a breach:
- Missing a required report
- Failing to provide receipts within the required time
- Failing to use the required storage system
- Failing to perform inspections on schedule
- Changing the inspection checklist without approval
- Withholding records after request
- Failing to document spending above the threshold
- Failing to report material property damage, tenant issues, or vacancy conditions

## 6) Record retention
All records must be kept for at least **[X years]** and stored in the approved system only.

Required records include:
- Photos and videos
- Inspection reports
- Meter readings
- Leases and notices
- Tenant communications
- Repair requests
- Invoices and receipts
- Vendor information
- Vacancy/turnover checklists
- Payment records
- Any approvals or denials

## 7) Storage rules
All records must be stored in **one approved system/folder**:
- No scattered texts
- No side emails unless copied into the approved system
- No private storage that the owner cannot access
- All files must be dated and labeled clearly

If a communication happens by text or email, it must be saved in the approved system.

## 8) Response time for backup requests
If the owner requests backup, the manager must respond within:
- **24 hours** for routine records
- **4 hours** for urgent matters
- **Immediately** for emergencies

Backup means the actual supporting record, not a summary.

## 9) Inspection standard
Every inspection must use the **same checklist**.

No improvising.

Each inspection should include:
- Same room order
- Same photo angles where possible
- Dated photos
- Meter readings if needed
- Notes on defects, wear, leaks, odors, pests, safety issues, and unauthorized changes

If the angle changes, the photo must still clearly show the same condition item being checked.

## 10) Spending authority
The manager may approve repairs up to: **$[amount]** without prior approval.

Anything above that requires written owner approval first, except true emergencies.

Emergency spending must be reported within **24 hours** with:
- Reason for emergency action
- Amount spent
- Vendor used
- Supporting receipt

## 11) Standard of compliance
The standard is not whether the property “seems fine.”
The standard is whether:
- records exist
- records are timely
- records are complete
- records are stored properly
- records can be produced quickly
- the same process is followed every time

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean one-page version** with blanks filled in for:
- report due dates
- spending threshold
- retention period
- response times
- breach consequences

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little generic in the enforcement part.

What I’d want added is the teeth — not a lecture, actual consequences.

- first missed report: written notice
- second miss or incomplete packet: management review / probation
- third miss: replacement or termination trigger
- failure to produce backup within the deadline: same thing
- unauthorized spending: not reimbursable unless approved after the fact, in writing

And I’d want the approval chain spelled out. Not just “owner approval,” but who can approve if I’m away, and what counts as approved — email only, no phone call nonsense unless it’s an emergency and then confirmed in writing after.

Also: one naming convention for files. Date_property_unit_item. Something like that. If they can’t keep files straight, they’re probably sloppy everywhere else.

On inspections, I’d want the checklist attached as an exhibit, not just referenced. Same list every time, same order. If there’s a change, it gets versioned and dated. No quiet edits.

And the storage rule should say who has access, not just where the files live. If I can’t open it myself or if somebody can delete stuff without a trace, that’s no good.

So yeah — draft it like a real operating standard with defaults, deadlines, breach steps, and access control. Not a friendly manager memo. That’s what I’d hand someone.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more enforceable version with actual consequences and control points.

---

# Rental Oversight Operating Standard

## 1) Purpose
This standard governs reporting, inspections, recordkeeping, approvals, spending, and document storage for the property.

Failure to follow this standard is a management failure.

---

## 2) Required recipients
All reports and supporting records must be sent to:

- Owner: [name/email]
- Backup approver: [name/email]
- Additional recipient(s), if any: [name/email]

A report is not considered delivered unless it is sent to all required recipients.

---

## 3) Approval chain
### Routine approvals
All non-emergency approvals require **written approval by email** from:

1. Owner: [name/email]
2. If owner is unavailable, backup approver: [name/email]

### Emergency approvals
If immediate action is required to prevent injury, major damage, or legal violation:
- Manager may act first
- Must notify owner and backup approver within **24 hours**
- Must provide written confirmation afterward
- Emergency spending is only reimbursable if the emergency was real and the action was reasonable

### No verbal-only approvals
Phone calls, texts, or in-person conversations do not count as approval unless confirmed in writing afterward.

---

## 4) Reporting schedule
### Monthly report
Due by: **[day] of each month**

Must include:
- Rent roll
- Delinquencies
- Open work orders
- Expenses
- Receipts
- Vacancies
- Lease issues
- Monthly exception report
- Any unresolved items from prior month

### Inspection report
Due within: **24 hours** of inspection

Must include:
- Same checklist every time
- Dated photos
- Notes on changes
- Meter readings if applicable

### Repair report
Due within: **48 hours** of completion

Must include:
- Issue reported
- Action taken
- Vendor used
- Cost
- Before/after photos
- Receipt

---

## 5) Missed or incomplete reports: enforcement
### First missed or incomplete report
- Written notice of deficiency

### Second missed or incomplete report
- Management review and probation notice

### Third missed or incomplete report
- Replacement or termination trigger

### Failure to produce backup within deadline
- Same escalation as a missed report

### Failure to follow storage or approval rules
- Treated as a report failure if it prevents verification

---

## 6) Spending authority
Manager may approve repairs up to: **$[amount]** without prior approval.

Anything above that requires written approval first, except true emergencies.

### Unauthorized spending
- Not reimbursable unless later approved in writing by owner
- If approval is denied, the expense remains the manager’s responsibility
- Repeated unauthorized spending is a breach and a termination trigger

---

## 7) Recordkeeping rules
### Required retention
All records must be kept for at least **[X years]**.

### Required records
- Photos and videos
- Inspection reports
- Meter readings
- Leases and notices
- Tenant communications
- Repair requests
- Invoices and receipts
- Vendor information
- Vacancy/turnover checklists
- Payment records
- Approvals and denials

### Storage system
All records must be stored in **one approved system/folder**.

No scattered records. No private side storage. No unshared folders.

### Access control
The owner must have:
- Full access
- View and download rights
- Ability to retrieve records without manager permission

No one may delete or alter records without leaving a trace.

---

## 8) File naming convention
All files must use this format:

**YYYY-MM-DD_Property_Unit_Item**

Examples:
- 2026-04-01_123Main_Unit2_Inspection
- 2026-04-03_123Main_Unit2_WaterHeaterReceipt
- 2026-04-05_123Main_Unit2_TenantNotice

If the file is a photo set, include the inspection or repair label in the folder name.

---

## 9) Inspection standard
### Attached checklist
The inspection checklist is attached as **Exhibit A** and controls every inspection.

### Rules
- Same checklist every time
- Same order every time
- Same room sequence every time
- Same photo angles where possible
- Any change to the checklist must be versioned, dated, and approved in writing

### Inspection findings must cover
- Damage
- Wear
- Leaks
- Odors
- Moisture
- Pests
- Safety issues
- Unauthorized changes
- Missing items
- Meter readings if vacant or relevant

---

## 10) Vacancy / turnover standard
If a unit becomes vacant, the following checklist is required:
- Locks and keys
- Smoke/CO detectors
- Leaks and moisture
- Odors
- Pests
- Appliances
- HVAC
- Flooring/carpet
- Walls/paint
- Trash removal
- Cleaning status
- Final meter readings
- Evidence of unauthorized access or damage

---

## 11) Response times for backup requests
If the owner requests backup, the manager must provide it within:

- **24 hours** for routine requests
- **4 hours** for urgent requests
- **Immediately** for emergencies

Backup means the actual record, not a summary.

---

## 12) Breach definitions
The following are breaches:
- Missing a required report
- Submitting an incomplete report
- Failing to use the approved storage system
- Failing to retain records
- Failing to follow the inspection checklist
- Changing the checklist without approval
- Failing to produce backup on time
- Spending above threshold without approval
- Withholding notices, receipts, or photos
- Failing to document material property issues or tenant issues

---

## 13) Standard of compliance
Compliance is measured by whether:
- records exist
- records are timely
- records are complete
- records are stored properly
- records are accessible to the owner
- the same process is followed every time

A verbal assurance does not satisfy this standard.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a cleaner **one-page manager agreement** with:
- blanks for names and dates
- a simple breach/escalation table
- Exhibit A as a reusable inspection checklist

user_simulator.text:
Closer. The bones are there, but I’d still want the enforcement table made more explicit — like a real contract, not just headings.

What’s missing is the exact action after each breach. Not “management review” in the abstract. I’d want:
- who issues the notice
- whether it has to be in writing only
- how many days to cure
- what happens if they don’t cure
- whether repeated failures void reimbursement or authority immediately

And I’d tighten the access part. Not just “full access” — I’d want:
- owner admin access
- backup approver access
- audit trail on changes and deletions
- no sole control by the manager

Also, the file naming thing should be mandatory, not just suggested. Same for the checklist version number. If Exhibit A changes, that version number should be part of the record.

So yeah, draft the next pass with a breach/remedy table and the access-control language spelled out. That’s the piece I’d actually hand somebody and expect them to live with.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter, more contractual version.

---

# Rental Oversight Operating Standard

## 1) Purpose
This standard governs reporting, inspections, recordkeeping, approvals, spending, and access control for the property.

Failure to comply is a management failure and may trigger cure, suspension of authority, non-reimbursement, or termination as stated below.

---

## 2) Required recipients
All reports, notices, and supporting records must be sent to:

- Owner: [name/email]
- Backup approver: [name/email]
- Additional recipient(s), if any: [name/email]

A report is not delivered unless sent to all required recipients.

---

## 3) Approval chain
### Routine approvals
All non-emergency approvals require **written approval by email** from:

1. Owner: [name/email]
2. If owner is unavailable, backup approver: [name/email]

### Emergency approvals
If immediate action is required to prevent injury, major damage, or legal violation:
- Manager may act first
- Manager must notify owner and backup approver within **24 hours**
- Written confirmation must follow
- Emergency spending is reimbursable only if the emergency was real and the action was reasonable

### No verbal-only approvals
Phone calls, texts, or in-person conversations do not count unless confirmed in writing afterward.

---

## 4) Reporting schedule
### Monthly report
Due by: **[day] of each month**

Must include:
- Rent roll
- Delinquencies
- Open work orders
- Expenses
- Receipts
- Vacancies
- Lease issues
- Monthly exception report
- Unresolved items from prior month

### Inspection report
Due within: **24 hours** of inspection

Must include:
- Same checklist every time
- Dated photos
- Notes on changes
- Meter readings if applicable

### Repair report
Due within: **48 hours** of completion

Must include:
- Issue reported
- Action taken
- Vendor used
- Cost
- Before/after photos
- Receipt

---

## 5) Breach, notice, cure, and remedy table

| Breach | Notice issued by | Notice form | Cure deadline | If not cured | Effect on authority / reimbursement |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Missed monthly report | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days | Second notice; counts as repeated failure | Repeated failure triggers probation; third failure triggers termination trigger |
| Incomplete monthly report | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days | Second notice; counts as repeated failure | Same as above |
| Missed inspection report | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days | Second notice; counts as repeated failure | Same as above |
| Failure to produce requested backup | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 24 hours routine / 4 hours urgent | Counts as missed report | Same escalation as missed report |
| Unauthorized spending under threshold | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days to explain and document | If not cured, expense is non-reimbursable | Repeated unauthorized spending suspends spending authority |
| Unauthorized spending over threshold | Owner or backup approver | Written only | No automatic cure | Immediate review | Not reimbursable unless later approved in writing |
| Failure to use approved storage system | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days | Counts as report failure if records cannot be verified | Repeated failure suspends authority |
| Failure to follow inspection checklist/version | Owner or backup approver | Written only | Immediate correction on next inspection | Counts as inspection failure | Repeated failure triggers probation |
| Withholding notices, receipts, or photos | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 24 hours | Immediate breach | Authority may be suspended immediately |
| Failure to document material property or tenant issue | Owner or backup approver | Written only | 2 business days | Counts as incomplete report | Repeated failure triggers termination trigger |

### Escalation sequence
- **First failure:** written notice
- **Second failure of same type:** probation notice
- **Third failure of same type:** replacement or termination trigger

### Immediate suspension triggers
The following may suspend authority immediately, without waiting for a third failure:
- Withholding records
- Unauthorized spending over threshold
- Falsifying or altering records
- Refusing access to owner or backup approver
- Repeated deletion or concealment of records

---

## 6) Spending authority
Manager may approve repairs up to: **$[amount]** without prior approval.

Anything above that requires written approval first, except true emergencies.

### Unauthorized spending
- Not reimbursable unless later approved in writing
- If approval is denied, the expense remains the manager’s responsibility
- Repeated unauthorized spending is a breach and may suspend spending authority immediately

---

## 7) Recordkeeping rules
### Required retention
All records must be kept for at least **[X years]**.

### Required records
- Photos and videos
- Inspection reports
- Meter readings
- Leases and notices
- Tenant communications
- Repair requests
- Invoices and receipts
- Vendor information
- Vacancy/turnover checklists
- Payment records
- Approvals and denials

### Storage system
All records must be stored in **one approved system/folder**.

No scattered records. No private side storage. No unshared folders.

### Access control
The owner must have:
- **Admin access**
- View and download rights
- Ability to retrieve records without manager permission

The backup approver must have:
- Access sufficient to view and retrieve all active records

### Audit trail
The storage system must preserve:
- Change history
- Upload history
- Deletion history
- User identity for edits or deletions, where supported by the system

### Control restriction
No single manager may have sole control of the records system.

No one may delete or alter records without an audit trail.

---

## 8) File naming convention
All files are mandatory named as follows:

**YYYY-MM-DD_Property_Unit_Item**

Examples:
- 2026-04-01_123Main_Unit2_Inspection
- 2026-04-03_123Main_Unit2_WaterHeaterReceipt
- 2026-04-05_123Main_Unit2_TenantNotice

### Checklist versioning
The inspection checklist must be labeled by version number, for example:
- Exhibit A v1.0
- Exhibit A v1.1

Any change to the checklist must be:
- dated
- versioned
- approved in writing
- saved with the prior version retained

---

## 9) Inspection standard
### Attached checklist
The inspection checklist is attached as **Exhibit A** and controls every inspection.

### Rules
- Same checklist every time
- Same order every time
- Same room sequence every time
- Same photo angles where possible
- Any change must be versioned and approved in writing

### Inspection findings must cover
- Damage
- Wear
- Leaks
- Odors
- Moisture
- Pests
- Safety issues
- Unauthorized changes
- Missing items
- Meter readings if vacant or relevant

---

## 10) Vacancy / turnover standard
If a unit becomes vacant, the following checklist is required:
- Locks and keys
- Smoke/CO detectors
- Leaks and moisture
- Odors
- Pests
- Appliances
- HVAC
- Flooring/carpet
- Walls/paint
- Trash removal
- Cleaning status
- Final meter readings
- Evidence of unauthorized access or damage

---

## 11) Response times for backup requests
If the owner requests backup, the manager must provide it within:

- **24 hours** for routine requests
- **4 hours** for urgent requests
- **Immediately** for emergencies

Backup means the actual record, not a summary.

---

## 12) Standard of compliance
Compliance is measured by whether:
- records exist
- records are timely
- records are complete
- records are stored properly
- records are accessible to the owner
- the same process is followed every time

A verbal assurance does not satisfy this standard.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean one-page agreement format** with:
- signature lines
- fill-in blanks
- Exhibit A inspection checklist
- a compact breach/remedy table for easy handoff

user_simulator.text:
That’s it. Finally looks like something with teeth.

The breach/remedy table is the part I wanted, and the access language is where most people get sloppy. Admin access, backup access, audit trail, no sole control — good. File naming mandatory, checklist versioned, written approvals only. That’s the standard.

I’d probably still want one hard line added somewhere: any deletion or alteration without the audit trail is automatic cause for removal, no cure. But as a working draft, this is close enough to hand somebody and see if they can live with it.

If you can make it into a clean one-page agreement with signature lines and Exhibit A, that’d be the useful next step.
